citations.content: Performance framing dominates.

parentBlock.content: • Performance framing dominates. Language that positions biometric data as performance optimization ― not health monitoring - activates identified and integrated regulation, the highest-quality extrinsic motivation in Self-Determination Theory (SDT). SDT-based interventions produce d=0.23 on autonomous motivation and sustained behavior change.

citations.content: Choice architecture outweighs word choice.

parentBlock.content: . Choice architecture outweighs word choice. In the only controlled digital health experiment testing autonomy-supportive vs. controlling language (Smit et al., 2019), language manipulation produced no significant main effects. But provision of choice significantly improved evaluation (b=.14, p=.003) and perceived relevance for high-autonomy users (b=.13, p=.02).²

parentBlock.content: - High performers exhibit amplified reactance. Trait reactance correlates with resistance to rules, high defensiveness, and lower therapy compliance.³

finding_title.value: Gain framing reduces reactance; loss framing can boomerang

citations.content: Gain framing reduces reactance; loss framing can boomerang.

parentBlock.content: - Gain framing reduces reactance; loss framing can boomerang. Gain-framed messages are generally perceived as less coercive and less freedom-threatening. However, for high self-efficacy / high-grit individuals, loss frames framed as opportunity cost ("here's what you're leaving on the table") can be more effective.4

citations.content: Metaphoric fit is non-negotiable.

parentBlock.content: - Metaphoric fit is non-negotiable. When a health risk is framed metaphorically (e.g., "tail risk"), the solution must also be framed in the same metaphorical domain. Mismatched metaphor-risk + literal-solution framing decreased prevention intentions below baseline in controlled experiments.5

finding_title.value: The 'data-as-mirror' technique is the optimal agent posture

citations.content: The "data-as-mirror" technique is the optimal agent posture.

parentBlock.content: . The "data-as-mirror" technique is the optimal agent posture. Present biometric data as a neutral, precise reflection of the user's own physiology, then step back. This activates the person's natural problem-solving instinct and positions them as analyst rather than patient. Combine with two clear options: "Two paths given today's data. Your call." "7

citations.content: Identity alignment:

parentBlock.content: . Identity alignment: A study in the Journal of Behavioral Medicine (2024) evaluated associations between exercise identity, behavioral regulations, and wearable device adherence. While the direct identity-adherence link was more complex than hypothesized, the key theoretical insight holds: when a device is experienced as identity-consistent (performance tool) rather than identity-inconsistent (health monitor), the psychological relationship with the technology shifts fundamentally.1

citations.content: SDT motivation quality:

parentBlock.content: • SDT motivation quality: Performance framing activates "identified regulation" and "integrated regulation" in Self-Determination Theory terms - the highest quality extrinsic motivation. The Ntoumanis et al. (2021) meta-analysis of 73 SDT-based interventions found that only increases in need support and autonomous motivation (not controlled motivation or amotivation) were associated with positive changes in health behavior. Interventions produced g=0.64 for need support and g=0.31 for competence at end of intervention.²

citations.content: Stigma avoidance:

parentBlock.content: - Stigma avoidance: High performers avoid health-as-problem-solving framing because it triggers a stigma response. Platforms like WHOOP and Oura sustain elite athlete engagement precisely by framing outputs as performance optimization rather than health monitoring. The language of "recovery score," "strain," and "readiness" keeps the user in athlete mode, not patient mode.

citations.content: Introjected motivation and wearables:

parentBlock.content: • Introjected motivation and wearables: A longitudinal study found that wearable technology use was associated with more controlled forms of motivation, specifically introjected motivation. Participants using wearables experienced a decline in physical activity compared to non-users over time. Qualitative data revealed participants felt guilt and internal pressure - motives consistent with controlled motivation - from wearable feedback. 4

citations.content: The risk-vs-diagnosis problem:

parentBlock.content: • The risk-vs-diagnosis problem: Alzheimer's biomarker communication research demonstrates the critical importance of language precision. Participants strongly prefer language that clarifies "this is a risk indicator, not a diagnosis." The disclosure process must ensure individuals understand the probabilistic nature of biomarker results. In a study from the Journal of Prevention of Alzheimer's Disease, 98% of participants could accurately convey their scan results when a structured, agency-preserving protocol was used.6

citations.content: The exception - data scientist framing:

parentBlock.content: • The exception - data scientist framing: When clinical precision is combined with autonomy-preserving language ("Your HRV is 2.40 below your personal baseline — here's what that could mean"), the relationship becomes "data scientist" rather than "doctor-patient." High performers respond to this because it activates their analytical identity rather than a patient identity.

overview.value: High-achieving, Type A individuals exhibit significantly higher psychological reactance to directive language. Steindl and colleagues document that Rhodewalt and Marcroft (1988) showed people with Type A personality (extreme achievement striving, time urgency) showed considerable reactance when a behavioral freedom was blocked, whereas Type Bs did not.

core_insight.value: High-achieving people have typically reached their position via intrinsic motivation and integrated regulation — behaviors so deeply aligned with identity that they feel natural, not forced. They have enormous psychological need for competence, autonomy, and agency. Health interventions that activate external regulation ('you should do X') or introjected regulation ('you'll feel bad if you don't') are likely to be rejected, minimized, or complied with resentfully — producing short-term behavior change but not sustained internalization.

citations.content: The core SDT insight for high performers: High-achieving people have typically reached their position via intrinsic motivation and integrated regulation - behaviors so deeply aligned with identity that they feel natural, not forced. They have enormous psychological need for competence, autonomy, and agency. Health interventions that activate external regulation ('you should do X') or introjected regulation ('you'll feel bad if you don't') are likely to be rejected, minimized, or complied with resentfully - producing short-term behavior change but not sustained internalization.

principle_description.value: Psychological Reactance Theory (Brehm, 1966): high trait reactance correlates with high autonomy need, resistance to rules, and defensiveness. Type A personality showed significantly greater reactance when behavioral freedom was blocked. Any language that implies 'you should do this' or 'you need to stop doing that' activates the boomerang effect — the user does the opposite.

principle.value: Default to performance framing. Every first-touch message should use the user's domain language (deal/training/capacity) before any health or clinical language enters.

principle.value: Always offer two options. Every recommendation the agent makes should include at least two paths. This satisfies the choice architecture finding from Smit et al. (2019) and reduces reactance.

principle.value: Never diagnose. The agent is a mirror and an analyst, never a doctor. Even when patterns are clinically concerning, the language should be: 'Here's what the data shows. This is the kind of pattern that [population norm]. You decide.'

principle.value: Use sigma notation, not color codes. High performers respond to statistical precision (σ below baseline, percentile rank) more than red/yellow/green traffic lights. Statistical language activates their analytical identity.

principle.value: Frame recovery as ROI, not rest. Recovery is a strategic input, not a concession to weakness. 'This is the highest-leverage action available to you right now' beats 'You need to rest.'

principle.value: Calibrate gain vs. loss framing to context. Default to gain framing for most interactions. Reserve opportunity-cost framing ('here's what you're leaving on the table') for users who have demonstrated high engagement and high self-efficacy.

principle.value: Match metaphor end-to-end. Per the metaphoric fit principle (Landau et al.), if the alert uses deal language, the recommendation must also use deal language. Never mix a finance-framed problem with a wellness-framed solution.

overview.value: This report synthesizes evidence across SDT, psychological reactance, message framing, and conceptual metaphor theory. The following limitations should inform how YU weights these findings.

citations.content: Population specificity gap.

parentBlock.content: . Population specificity gap. Most SDT research is conducted on general populations (university students, primary care patients), not specifically Type A or high-achieving professionals. The application of SDT motivation taxonomy to finance professionals and elite athletes is theoretically grounded but not directly validated in controlled trials with these populations.

citations.content: Framing heterogeneity.

parentBlock.content: • Framing heterogeneity. Gain vs. loss framing literature is highly heterogeneous. Recent meta-analyses (2025, 2026) find no significant overall differences in physical activity attitudes, intentions, or behaviors between gain- and loss-framed messages. Individual moderators (self-efficacy, grit, consideration of future consequences) predict framing response better than demographic categories. 1,2

citations.content: Metaphor research scope.

parentBlock.content: • Metaphor research scope. The metaphoric fit principle (Landau et al.) is largely demonstrated on physical health risks (skin cancer, sun exposure). The principle has not been directly tested in wearable/performance optimization contexts. The extension to finance and athletics metaphor domains is a reasonable inference from CMT but lacks direct experimental validation.³

citations.content: Effect size reality.

parentBlock.content: • Effect size reality. Most SDT intervention effect sizes are small-to-medium (d=0.23, or d=0.15 after correction for publication bias). Language framing is one input among many. Content quality, delivery timing, personalization depth, and user context all contribute to engagement outcomes. Language framing sets the ceiling; these other factors determine how close you get to it." 4

citations.content: Wearable-identity interaction.

parentBlock.content: - Wearable-identity interaction. The expected finding that exercise identity predicts wearable adherence was not supported in the most relevant study (J Behavioral Medicine, 2024). The authors found device wear adherence was largely independent of identity and motivation. This suggests the identity-alignment benefit of performance framing may operate at the level of engagement quality (how deeply users process and act on data) rather than wear adherence (whether they keep the device on).5

citations.content: SDT-based interventions produce d=0.23 on autonomous motivation and sustained behavior change

parentBlock.content: • Performance framing dominates. Language that positions biometric data as performance optimization ― not health monitoring - activates identified and integrated regulation, the highest-quality extrinsic motivation in Self-Determination Theory (SDT). SDT-based interventions produce d=0.23 on autonomous motivation and sustained behavior change.

parentBlock.content: . Choice architecture outweighs word choice. parentBlock.content: • SDT motivation quality: Performance framing activates "identified regulation" and "integrated regulation" in Self-Determination Theory terms - the highest quality extrinsic motivation. The Ntoumanis et al. (2021) meta-analysis of 73 SDT-based interventions found that only increases in need support and autonomous motivation (not controlled motivation or amotivation) were associated with positive changes in health behavior. Interventions produced g=0.64 for need support and g=0.31 for competence at end of intervention.²

statistic.value: 98% of participants could accurately convey their scan results when a structured, agency-preserving protocol was used

citations.content: 98% of participants could accurately convey their scan results when a structured, agency-preserving protocol was used

parentBlock.content: • The risk-vs-diagnosis problem: Alzheimer's biomarker communication research demonstrates the critical importance of language precision. Participants strongly prefer language that clarifies "this is a risk indicator, not a diagnosis." The disclosure process must ensure individuals understand the probabilistic nature of biomarker results. In a study from the Journal of Prevention of Alzheimer's Disease, 98% of participants could accurately convey their scan results when a structured, agency-preserving protocol was used.6
